scenario: A registration form has an "Email" field that must be required -- users can't submit the form without filling it. The team also wants the form's UI to visually indicate which fields are mandatory (typically a red asterisk next to the label).
question: Which Form field property handles required validation AND triggers the visual indicator?

options.text: Manually append an asterisk ` to the field's label text -- for example, changing "Email" to "Email ". This creates the visual appearance of a required field indicator but provides no actual validation: users can still submit the form with the email field left blank, and the form renderer has no mechanism to block the submission. This approach also splits responsibility: the visual cue is in the label while validation logic is absent, creating false confidence for users who assume the asterisk means their input will be checked. The Required` validation property handles both behaviours with a single setting.

Configure a custom CSS theme that highlights or marks required fields visually -- for example, adding a red border or asterisk via CSS selectors targeting empty fields. CSS operates at the presentation layer and has no ability to block form submission or enforce validation rules. Even using CSS pseudo-classes like :invalid or :required would only provide browser-level hints, not enforced Camunda form validation. The Camunda Forms renderer manages its own styling based on the Carbon design system; no CSS theme hook exists for automatically adding required field indicators from a theme alone.

Camunda Forms does not support required field validation -- all fields are always optional, and enforcing mandatory inputs requires writing custom submit-time validation code outside of the form renderer. This claim is inaccurate. Required is one of the most fundamental validation properties in the Camunda Forms element library, available on Textfield, Number, Datetime, Checkbox group, Radio, Select, and Taglist elements (documented in each element's library specification).

"). The Form renderer applies the validation at submit time (rejecting empty inputs) and the renderer adds an asterisk indicator next to the field label automatically. One property handles both behaviours.

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda Forms' validation property required does double duty: (1) at submit time, the form rejects empty values and shows an error message; (2) at render time, the field gets the required indicator (typically an asterisk) next to the label. This is the canonical, single-source-of-truth way to express mandatory fields. The indicator is automatic -- no manual styling needed.

b.text: Incorrect. Adding * to the label visually communicates "required" but doesn't actually enforce. Users see the indicator, submit empty, form accepts -- defeats the contract. Always pair visual cues with real validation.

c.text: Incorrect. CSS handles visual styling, not validation. Even if CSS detected empty fields (via :invalid), it can't reject submission.

d.text: Correct. validate.required is a foundational property.
